aliases: Vanilla ice cream drowned in espresso

english: Vanilla ice cream drowned in espresso

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for affogato (ice cream + espresso composite). Mapped to ice cream food product as the dominant component. Espresso component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for affogato. A00JN (Ice cream) used as the closest defensible anchor for the dominant component. Espresso component unrepresented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for affogato or composite ice cream dessert with espresso.

serving.description: 1 serving (1 scoop vanilla ice cream + 1 shot espresso)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of affogato consists of approximately 150 g, comprising one scoop of vanilla ice cream (approximately 100 g) topped with one shot of freshly brewed espresso (approximately 30–50 ml).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ice Creams, vanilla (SR Legacy ID 19095) and Beverages, coffee, espresso, restaurant-prepared (SR Legacy ID 14209)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Affogato is a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by blending USDA SR Legacy data for vanilla ice cream (approximately 67% by weight, ~100 g of 150 g serving) and brewed espresso (approximately 33% by weight, ~50 g of 150 g serving). The resulting per-100 g composite is consistent with the input record values and has been cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo data for ice cream desserts. Input record values (energy 120 kcal, protein 2.33 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 12.0 g) are plausible and retained with minor rounding adjustments.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for affogato. GI estimated at 61 based on vanilla ice cream GI of approximately 57–62 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The espresso component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (61 × 18.0) / 100 = 11.0, rounded to 10.8 using 17.7 g available carbohydrate (12.0 g/100 g × 150 g × 0.985 adjustment for fibre). Medium GI category (56–69).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.67 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.33 g/100 g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness despite low sodium (40 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit affogato to a small occasional portion (≤75 g) as the high sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and the saturated fat content may worsen arterial compliance; a reduced-sugar or lower-fat ice cream alternative is preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 10.67 g/100 g and total carbohydrate at 12.0 g/100 g with minimal fibre (0.33 g/100 g) produce a rapid postprandial glucose rise; medium GI (~61) and low fibre content offer limited glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat affogato as an occasional indulgence only, limiting portion to ≤50 g, pairing with a protein-rich meal to blunt glucose response, and considering a sugar-free or reduced-sugar ice cream substitute to lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g from vanilla ice cream raise LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content is 23.33 mg/100 g; high sugar (10.67 g/100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit affogato to small occasional portions (≤75 g) due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and high sugar content, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and cardiovascular risk; choosing a low-fat, low-sugar frozen dessert alternative is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Affogato (Italian: 'drowned') is a classic Italian dessert-beverage hybrid consisting of one or two scoops of cold vanilla gelato or ice cream submerged in a freshly pulled single or double shot of hot espresso. The name derives from the Italian verb 'affogare' (to drown), describing the ice cream being drowned in espresso. The dish is assembled rather than cooked: the espresso is brewed at high pressure and poured directly over the ice cream at the moment of serving, creating a dramatic contrast of temperatures — hot espresso against frozen ice cream — and a contrast of flavours — bitter, roasted coffee against sweet, creamy vanilla. As the espresso melts the ice cream, a rich, semi-liquid sauce forms that blends the two components. Texturally, affogato transitions from a cold, firm scoop to a warm, creamy, partially melted pool within minutes of assembly. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 180 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 10 g fat (6 g saturated), 18 g carbohydrate, and 16 g sugar. The high sugar and saturated fat content make it a high-concern food for type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease management. The espresso component contributes caffeine (approximately 60–80 mg per shot) and chlorogenic acid polyphenols with mild antioxidant properties, but these benefits are outweighed by the ice cream's sugar and saturated fat load. Affogato is widely served in Italian restaurants, cafés, and dessert bars globally, including in Singapore where it appears on specialty coffee and dessert menus. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but not vegan or dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Can I eat affogato if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Affogato is a composite dish with no single approved-source entry. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for vanilla ice cream and espresso, blended by estimated weight ratio (67:33), and cross-checked against the input record. The input values are plausible and consistent with USDA component data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database vanilla ice cream entry. Confidence is medium because no exact composite entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the affogato composite is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the blended dish; individual ice cream fatty acid data exists but was not applied to avoid over-precision in a composite estimate. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite affogato dish are not reported in the primary approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo) at the composite level; individual component data exists but blended composite values are not published. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: vanilla ice cream and espresso contain negligible to zero vitamin C; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for both components. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g/100 g: small amount from dairy fat in vanilla ice cream (naturally occurring trans fat, vaccenic acid); value retained from input record and consistent with USDA data for full-fat ice cream. Affogato is of Italian origin; it is served widely in Singapore and internationally. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' which reflects the serving context (GrabFood Singapore) rather than culinary origin; corrected to Italy in this record. The input ethnic_tags included 'Pasta' which is erroneous for affogato and has been removed. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (61 × 18.0 g carbohydrate per 150 g serving) / 100 = 10.98, reported as 10.8 after adjusting for available carbohydrate net of fibre (0.5 g fibre × 150/100 = 0.5 g fibre per serving; available carb = 18.0 − 0.5 = 17.7 g; GL = 61 × 17.7 / 100 = 10.8).
